1: Phēstos oun epibas tē eparcheia (Phēstos oun epibas tē eparcheia): 'Festus then, having entered upon the province'. Epibainō (step into/take charge of) is the standard term for a governor's formal assumption of duties. Festus's three-day journey to Jerusalem before going to Caesarea signals correct protocol — he visits the provincial capital's leading community before settling in the administrative center. The visit immediately exposes him to the Jewish leadership's agenda against Paul.
2: enephanisan de auto hoi archiereis kai hoi prōtoi ton Ioudaiōn kata tou Paulou: 'the chief priests and the leading men of the Jews brought charges against Paul'. Emphanizō (inform/lay charges) is the technical legal term for formal accusation — they are initiating judicial process, not merely complaining. The two-year imprisonment (24:27) has not diminished the establishment's hostility; their first act with the new governor is to renew the prosecution.
3: aitoumenoi charin kat autou hopōs metapempsētai auton eis Hierousalem: 'requesting a favor against him — that he would transfer him to Jerusalem'. Charin aitoumenoi (requesting a favor) is the language of patronage — they are asking Festus to do them a political kindness. The reason Luke makes clear: an ambush was planned (enedran poiountes anelein auton kata tēn hodon). The judicial request is cover for assassination; Festus's refusal to comply protects Paul providentially.
4: ho men oun Phēstos apekrithē tērousthai ton Paulon eis Kaisareian: Festus's procedurally correct response — Paul will be held in Caesarea (where Roman administrative infrastructure exists) and the case heard there. Apokrinomai (answer/decide) is the verb of judicial ruling. Festus is not sympathetic to Paul; he is following Roman legal principle: prisoners are tried at the place of custody, not transferred to accusers' jurisdictions.
5: hoi oun en hymin dynatoi synkatabantes (hoi oun en hymin dynatoi synkatabantes): 'let the powerful among you come down with me'. Dynatoi (powerful/capable men) — the civic and religious leaders who are competent to represent the prosecution. Festus invites them to Caesarea to present their case formally; this is fair-trial procedure. The phrase ei ti estin en tō andri atopon (if there is anything improper about the man) already frames Paul's guilt as unproven.
6: diatribas de en autois hēmeras ou pleious oktō e deka: 'having stayed among them not more than eight or ten days'. The precision — eight or ten — reflects the 'we' source's detailed itinerary notes. Festus spent minimal time in Jerusalem, consistent with a new governor eager to establish authority in the administrative center. His rapid departure to Caesarea and next-day judicial hearing show administrative efficiency.
7: polla kai barea aitīamata katapherontes (polla kai barea aitīamata katapherontes): 'bringing many and serious charges'. Barea (heavy/serious) is the legal weight of the accusations — capital offenses require serious evidentiary grounding. Ha ouk ischyon apodeixai (which they were unable to prove): Luke's legal verdict on the prosecution. The charges were numerous but unsubstantiated — the Roman judicial standard of proof was not met.
8: oute eis ton nomon tōn Ioudaiōn oute eis to hieron oute eis Kaisara ti hēmarton: Paul's threefold denial covering the three jurisdictions: Jewish law, the temple, and Roman imperial law. The triple structure is a formal legal defense addressing the three areas of accusation. Hēmarton (I sinned/offended) is the common-law vocabulary — Paul claims innocence on all three counts simultaneously.
9: ho Phēstos de thelōn tois Ioudaiois charin katathesthai (ho Phēstos de thelōn tois Ioudaiois charin katathesthai): 'Festus, wanting to do the Jews a favor'. Charin katathesthai (deposit a favor) is the patronage-accumulation vocabulary of Roman political life — Festus is banking goodwill with the Jewish leadership at Paul's expense. His suggestion that Paul be tried in Jerusalem represents judicial compromise that abandons procedural fairness for political advantage.
10: epi tou bēmatos Kaisaros hestōs eimi hou me dei krinesthai (epi tou bēmatos Kaisaros hestōs eimi hou me dei krinesthai): 'I am standing before Caesar's tribunal where I ought to be tried'. Bēma (judgment seat) is the elevated platform of Roman justice; Paul is asserting that standing before a Roman governor's tribunal is standing before Caesar's authority. Dei (it is necessary) — Paul claims his proper jurisdiction as a Roman citizen is the imperial court, not a Jerusalem assembly where the accusers control the venue.
11: ei men adikō kai axion thanatou pepracha ti ou paraitoumai to apothanein... Kaisara epikaloumai: 'if I am doing wrong and have done something deserving death, I do not beg off from dying... I appeal to Caesar'. Kaisara epikaloumai is the juridical formula of provocatio ad Caesarem — the appeal by which Roman citizens claimed imperial jurisdiction over their case. The two-part statement is rhetorically precise: Paul accepts the death penalty in principle if guilty; but since the charges are false, he appeals to the highest court. Once spoken, this formula was legally binding.
12: Kaisara epikeklēsai, epi Kaisara poreusē (Kaisara epikeklēsai, epi Kaisara poreusē): 'you have appealed to Caesar — to Caesar you will go'. Festus's ruling, after consulting with the council (symboulion), accepts the appeal. The terse formulation mirrors the juridical terseness of the original appeal. Luke records the irony: Festus, trying to please the Jewish leadership, has instead determined that Paul will reach Rome — the very destination the Lord promised in 23:11.
13: Agrippos de ho basileus kai Bernikē katēntēsan eis Kaisareian aspasamenoi ton Phēston: Herod Agrippa II (son of Agrippa I, great-grandson of Herod the Great) and his sister Bernice arrive to pay respects to the new governor. Agrippa ruled territories northeast of Galilee; his sister Bernice was at this time a widow living with her brother (the relationship was regarded with suspicion by Roman writers including Josephus). Their arrival provides the occasion for Paul's most elaborate defense speech.
14: ho Phēstos tō basilei anethet ta kata ton Paulon: 'Festus laid before the king the matters concerning Paul'. Anatithēmi (lay before/refer) is the consultation-vocabulary — Festus is seeking Agrippa's help with a case he finds legally opaque. The extended briefing (vv.14-21) reveals Festus's honest puzzlement: a prisoner whose accusers only raise religious disputes and who claims a dead man is alive.
15: peri hou genomenou mou eis Hierosolyma enephanisan hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi: Festus's report recaps the Jerusalem visit from the Jewish leadership's perspective — they demanded judgment against Paul. Enephanisan is the same formal accusation-term of v.2. Festus accurately represents the prosecution's urgency; his own judicial response (vv.16-17) shows he understood his legal obligation even while considering accommodating them.
16: pros ous apekrithēn hoti ouk estin ethos Rōmaiois charizesthai tina anthrōpon eis apōleian: 'to them I replied that it is not the custom of Romans to hand over any person as a favor to destruction'. Charizesthai... eis apōleian (give someone over to destruction) — the Roman legal principle that accusers must first have the opportunity to face the accused and make their charge (kata prosōpon echein ton katēgoroumenon kai topon apologia labein peri tou enklēmatos). This is due process in Roman law: confrontation and opportunity to defend.
17: synagentōn oun autōn enthade anabolēn mēdemian poiēsamenos: 'when they gathered here I made no delay'. Anabolē (delay/postponement) — Festus emphasizes his procedural promptness; the next day after arrival he convened the court. This detail establishes Festus as acting in good faith judicially, which makes the subsequent offer of Jerusalem jurisdiction more clearly a political rather than legal choice.
18: peri hou stathentes hoi katēgoroi oudemian aitian epheron hōn hypenooum egō ponērōn: 'about whom the accusers, when they stood up, brought no charge of the evil things I supposed'. Hypenooum (I supposed/suspected) — Festus expected criminal charges; Roman provinces dealt with banditry, sedition, murder. Instead the accusers raised religious disputes. Oudemian aitian... ponērōn (no charge of evils) is the fourth acquittal-formulation in Acts' judicial sequence (Lysias: 23:29; Festus: 25:18; Agrippa: 26:31-32).
19: zētēmata de tina peri tēs idias deisidaimonias eichon pros auton (zētēmata de tina peri tēs idias deisidaimonias eichon pros auton): 'they had certain disputes with him about their own religion/superstition'. Deisidaimonia (religion/superstition) is Festus's neutral administrative term — neither endorsing nor condemning. Peri tinos Iēsou tethnēkotos hon ephascen ho Paulos zēn: 'about a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul claimed to be alive'. Luke captures Festus's outsider summary of the resurrection claim: the entire dispute centers on whether a dead man is alive. The simplicity of the formulation is striking — all of Christian theology hangs on this question.
20: aporoumenos de egō tēn peri toutōn zētēsin (aporoumenos de egō tēn peri toutōn zētēsin): 'being at a loss about the investigation of these matters'. Aporeomai (be at a loss/perplexed) — Festus has no legal category for a dispute about resurrection. Elegon ei bouloito poreuesthai eis Hierosolyma: Festus presents his Jerusalem suggestion as an administrative solution to his own confusion, not as a favor to the accusers. The honest self-presentation before Agrippa reveals the judicial impasse that Paul's appeal resolves.
21: tou de Paulou epikalesamenos tērēthēnai auton eis tēn tou Sebastou diagnōsin: 'Paul having appealed to be held for the examination of the Augustus'. Sebastos is the Greek equivalent of Latin Augustus — the imperial title. Diagnōsis (examination/hearing) is the judicial term for a formal imperial inquiry. Festus's use of the technical vocabulary confirms he understands the legal force of Paul's appeal; it is now an imperial matter, and he is obligated to forward it with appropriate documentation.
22: Agrippos de pros ton Phēston, Eboylomēn kai autos tou anthrōpou akousai: 'Agrippa said to Festus: I myself also wanted to hear this man'. Agrippa's interest is genuine — he is an expert in Jewish affairs (v.26 confirms this), and the resurrection controversy would be theologically significant to him as a king whose subjects included many Jews. Aurion (tomorrow) — the immediate scheduling shows Agrippa's priority.
23: meta pollēs phantasias (meta pollēs phantasias): 'with great pomp/display'. Phantasia (show/pomp) is a NT hapax — a Hellenistic word for spectacular ceremony. Luke's description of Agrippa and Bernice entering the audience hall with military tribunes and leading men of the city (andras tous kat exochēn tēs poleōs) sets a deliberate theatrical contrast with Paul — the prisoner will address the most distinguished assembly in Acts' trial sequence. The scene mirrors imperial courtroom pageantry.
24: Agrippe basileu kai pantes hoi symparedontes hēmin andres: Festus's formal address to the assembled hearing. The public framing — 'all men present with us' (pantes hoi symparedontes hēmin andres) — confirms this is not a private inquiry but a quasi-official hearing with civic witnesses. Peri hou hapan to plēthos tōn Ioudaiōn enetychon moi (concerning whom the entire multitude of the Jews approached me): Festus represents the prosecution as a community-wide complaint, not a factional grievance.
25: egō de katelabon mēden axion autōn thanatou pepracthenai: 'I found that he had done nothing deserving death'. This is Festus's formal innocence verdict — the clearest judicial formulation he has given. The repetition of axios thanatou (worthy of death) from Paul's own speech (v.11) creates the irony: Paul acknowledged the principle; Festus confirms the evidence does not meet it. Autou de toutou epikalesamenos ton Sebaston — the appeal has forced the case to Caesar despite the lack of a capital charge.
26: peri hou asphales ti grapsai tō kyriō ouk echō (peri hou asphales ti grapsai tō kyriō ouk echō): 'about whom I have nothing certain to write to the lord [the emperor]'. Asphales (certain/secure/definite) — Festus's dilemma: Roman administrative procedure required a written statement of charges (elogium) to accompany a prisoner transferred to the emperor. Without definite charges, the document cannot be written. Agrippa's hearing is convened precisely to help Festus articulate what Paul is actually accused of.
27: alogon gar moi dokei pemponta desmion mē kai tas kat autou aitias sēmainein: 'it seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner without indicating the charges against him'. Alogon (unreasonable/irrational) — the administrative logic is clear: transferring a prisoner without charges is procedurally incoherent. Festus needs Agrippa's expertise in Jewish religious affairs to help him formulate the accusation. The hearing of ch. 26 is thus an administrative consultation, which Paul transforms into his most comprehensive missionary address.
